revision.text:
{{Short description|Ritual enshrinement of a Kami}} {{for|the Buddhist ritual (灌頂)| Abhiseka}} [[File:Kanjo ritual implements, Kamakura-Edo period, 13th-17th century, crown - Tokyo National Museum - DSC05208.JPG|thumb|right|''Kanjō'' ritual crown, Kamakura-Edo period (13th-17th century)]]
{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a [[Nyorai|buddha]] or [[:en:Bosatsu|bodhisattva]] to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a [[shinto shrine|shrine]] to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a [[:en:Kitsune|fox]] hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to [[Ono no Takamura]]'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ([[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori]]) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to [[Kyoto]], and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At [[Toyokawa Inari]], the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==References== ===Citations=== {{Reflist|2}} ===Bibliography=== * [[:en:Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2275 The Traditional Festival in Urban Society], 1975, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/search.php?type=1&term=Bunrei&andor=AND Bunrei], Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved on July 8, 2008

{{Short description|Ritual enshrinement of a Kami}} {{for|the Buddhist ritual (灌頂)| Abhiseka}} [[File:Kanjo ritual implements, Kamakura-Edo period, 13th-17th century, crown - Tokyo National Museum - DSC05208.JPG|thumb|right|''Kanjō'' ritual crown, Kamakura-Edo period (13th-17th century)]]
{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a [[:en:Nyorai|buddha]] or [[:en:Bosatsu|bodhisattva]] to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a [[shinto shrine|shrine]] to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a [[:en:Kitsune|fox]] hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to [[:en:Ono no Takamura|Ono no Takamura]]'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ([[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori]]) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At [[Toyokawa Inari]], the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==References== ===Citations=== {{Reflist|2}} ===Bibliography=== * [[:en:Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2275 The Traditional Festival in Urban Society], 1975, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/search.php?type=1&term=Bunrei&andor=AND Bunrei], Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved on July 8, 2008
== External links == {{Commons Category|Kanjō}} {{Shinto shrine}}
{{translated page|ja|分霊|version=104331971|comment=Imported full ja history}}

}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q195793]] Wikidata name: Bunrei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q195793]] Wikidata name: Bunrei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ritual enshrinement of a Kami}} {{for|the Buddhist ritual (灌頂)| Abhiseka}} [[File:Kanjo ritual implements, Kamakura-Edo period, 13th-17th century, crown - Tokyo National Museum - DSC05208.JPG|thumb|right|''Kanjō'' ritual crown, Kamakura-Edo period (13th-17th century)]]
{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a [[:en:Nyorai|buddha]] or [[:en:Bosatsu|bodhisattva]] to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a [[:en:Kitsune|fox]] hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to [[:en:Ono no Takamura|Ono no Takamura]]'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ([[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori]]) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]], the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==References== ===Citations=== {{Reflist|2}} ===Bibliography=== * [[:en:Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2275 The Traditional Festival in Urban Society], 1975, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/search.php?type=1&term=Bunrei&andor=AND Bunrei], Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved on July 8, 2008

[[en:Kanjō]] [[ja:分霊]] [[Category:Taoism]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Bunrei]] [[es:Bunrei]] [[fi:Bunrei]] [[fr:Bunrei]] [[id:Bunrei]] [[zh:分靈]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kanjō}} [[fi:Kanjō]] [[id:Kanjō]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q195793}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q195793]] Wikidata name: Bunrei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q195793]] Wikidata name: Bunrei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ritual enshrinement of a Kami}} {{for|the Buddhist ritual (灌頂)| Abhiseka}} [[File:Kanjo ritual implements, Kamakura-Edo period, 13th-17th century, crown - Tokyo National Museum - DSC05208.JPG|thumb|right|''Kanjō'' ritual crown, Kamakura-Edo period (13th-17th century)]]
{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a [[:en:Nyorai|buddha]] or [[:en:Bosatsu|bodhisattva]] to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a [[:en:Kitsune|fox]] hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to [[:en:Ono no Takamura|Ono no Takamura]]'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ([[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori]]) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]], the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==References== ===Citations=== {{Reflist|2}} ===Bibliography=== * [[:en:Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2275 The Traditional Festival in Urban Society], 1975, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/search.php?type=1&term=Bunrei&andor=AND Bunrei], Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved on July 8, 2008

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kanjō]][[en:Kanjō]][[fi:Kanjō]][[id:Kanjō]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q195793]] Wikidata name: Bunrei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q195793]] Wikidata name: Bunrei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ritual enshrinement of a Kami}} {{for|the Buddhist ritual (灌頂)| Abhiseka}} [[File:Kanjo ritual implements, Kamakura-Edo period, 13th-17th century, crown - Tokyo National Museum - DSC05208.JPG|thumb|right|''Kanjō'' ritual crown, Kamakura-Edo period (13th-17th century)]]
{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a [[:en:Nyorai|buddha]] or [[:en:Bosatsu|bodhisattva]] to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a [[:en:Kitsune|fox]] hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to [[:en:Ono no Takamura|Ono no Takamura]]'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ([[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori]]) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]], the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==References== ===Citations=== {{Reflist|2}} ===Bibliography=== * [[:en:Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2275 The Traditional Festival in Urban Society], 1975, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/search.php?type=1&term=Bunrei&andor=AND Bunrei], Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved on July 8, 2008

In 1194, [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. At [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]], the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kanjō]][[en:Kanjō]][[fi:Kanjō]][[id:Kanjō]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q195793]] Wikidata name: Bunrei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q195793]] Wikidata name: Bunrei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Ritual enshrinement of a Kami}} {{for|the Buddhist ritual (灌頂)| Abhiseka}} [[File:Kanjo ritual implements, Kamakura-Edo period, 13th-17th century, crown - Tokyo National Museum - DSC05208.JPG|thumb|right|''Kanjō'' ritual crown, Kamakura-Edo period (13th-17th century)]]
{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a [[:en:Nyorai|buddha]] or [[:en:Bosatsu|bodhisattva]] to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a [[:en:Kitsune|fox]] hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to [[:en:Ono no Takamura|Ono no Takamura]]'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ([[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori]]) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]], the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a [[:en:Nyorai|buddha]] or [[:en:Bosatsu|bodhisattva]] to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a [[:en:Kitsune|fox]] hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to [[:en:Ono no Takamura|Ono no Takamura]]'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ([[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori]]) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]], the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==References== ===Citations=== {{Reflist|2}} ===Bibliography=== * [[:en:Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2275 The Traditional Festival in Urban Society], 1975, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/search.php?type=1&term=Bunrei&andor=AND Bunrei], Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved on July 8, 2008

{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a [[:en:Nyorai|buddha]] or [[:en:Bosatsu|bodhisattva]] to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a [[:en:Kitsune|fox]] hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to [[:en:Ono no Takamura|Ono no Takamura]]'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ([[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori]]) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At [[:en:Toyokawa Inari|Toyokawa Inari]], the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}} is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}} to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a {{ill|Kitsune|en|Kitsune|WD=Q692111|lt=fox}} hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}} ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to {{ill|Ono no Takamura|en|Ono no Takamura|WD=Q1139928|lt=Ono no Takamura}}'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ({{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori}}) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At {{ill|Toyokawa Inari|en|Toyokawa Inari|WD=Q3080596|lt=Toyokawa Inari}}, the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==References== ===Citations=== {{Reflist|2}} ===Bibliography=== * {{ill|Karen Ann Smyers|en|Karen Ann Smyers|WD=Q6369485|lt=Smyers, Karen Ann}}. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2275 The Traditional Festival in Urban Society], 1975, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/search.php?type=1&term=Bunrei&andor=AND Bunrei], Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved on July 8, 2008

{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the {{ill|The Buddha|en|The Buddha|WD=Q9441|lt=The Buddha}}'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a {{ill|Nyorai|en|Nyorai|WD=Q17052684|lt=buddha}} or {{ill|Bosatsu|en|Bosatsu|WD=Q23925162|lt=bodhisattva}} to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}} is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}} to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a {{ill|Kitsune|en|Kitsune|WD=Q692111|lt=fox}} hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}} ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to {{ill|Ono no Takamura|en|Ono no Takamura|WD=Q1139928|lt=Ono no Takamura}}'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ({{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori}}) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At {{ill|Toyokawa Inari|en|Toyokawa Inari|WD=Q3080596|lt=Toyokawa Inari}}, the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

In 1194, {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At {{ill|Toyokawa Inari|en|Toyokawa Inari|WD=Q3080596|lt=Toyokawa Inari}}, the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2275 The Traditional Festival in Urban Society], 1975, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/search.php?type=1&term=Bunrei&andor=AND Bunrei], Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved on July 8, 2008

{{Short description|Ritual enshrinement of a Kami}} {{for|the Buddhist ritual (灌頂)| Abhiseka}} [[File:Kanjo ritual implements, Kamakura-Edo period, 13th-17th century, crown - Tokyo National Museum - DSC05208.JPG|thumb|right|''Kanjō'' ritual crown, Kamakura-Edo period (13th-17th century)]]
{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the {{ill|The Buddha|en|The Buddha|WD=Q9441|lt=The Buddha}}'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a {{ill|Nyorai|en|Nyorai|WD=Q17052684|lt=buddha}} or {{ill|Bosatsu|en|Bosatsu|WD=Q23925162|lt=bodhisattva}} to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}} is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}} to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a {{ill|Kitsune|en|Kitsune|WD=Q692111|lt=fox}} hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}} ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to {{ill|Ono no Takamura|en|Ono no Takamura|WD=Q1139928|lt=Ono no Takamura}}'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ({{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori}}) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At {{ill|Toyokawa Inari|en|Toyokawa Inari|WD=Q3080596|lt=Toyokawa Inari}}, the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==References== ===Citations=== {{Reflist|2}} ===Bibliography=== * {{ill|Karen Ann Smyers|en|Karen Ann Smyers|WD=Q6369485|lt=Smyers, Karen Ann}}. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2275 The Traditional Festival in Urban Society], 1975, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/search.php?type=1&term=Bunrei&andor=AND Bunrei], Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved on July 8, 2008

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kanjō]][[en:Kanjō]][[fi:Kanjō]][[id:Kanjō]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kanjō]][[en:Kanjō]][[fi:Kanjō]][[id:Kanjō]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kanjō]][[en:Kanjō]][[fi:Kanjō]][[id:Kanjō]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kanjō]][[en:Kanjō]][[fi:Kanjō]][[id:Kanjō]]
{{Short description|Ritual enshrinement of a Kami}} {{for|the Buddhist ritual (灌頂)| Abhiseka}} [[File:Kanjo ritual implements, Kamakura-Edo period, 13th-17th century, crown - Tokyo National Museum - DSC05208.JPG|thumb|right|''Kanjō'' ritual crown, Kamakura-Edo period (13th-17th century)]]
{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the {{ill|The Buddha|en|The Buddha|WD=Q9441|lt=The Buddha|1=The Buddha}}'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a {{ill|Nyorai|en|Nyorai|WD=Q17052684|lt=buddha|1=Nyorai}} or {{ill|Bosatsu|en|Bosatsu|WD=Q23925162|lt=bodhisattva|1=Bosatsu}} to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}} is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a {{ill|Kitsune|en|Kitsune|WD=Q692111|lt=fox|1=Kitsune}} hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}} ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to {{ill|Ono no Takamura|en|Ono no Takamura|WD=Q1139928|lt=Ono no Takamura|1=Ono no Takamura}}'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ({{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori|1=Aomori Prefecture}}) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At {{ill|Toyokawa Inari|en|Toyokawa Inari|WD=Q3080596|lt=Toyokawa Inari|1=Toyokawa Inari}}, the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==References== ===Citations=== {{Reflist|2}} ===Bibliography=== * {{ill|Karen Ann Smyers|en|Karen Ann Smyers|WD=Q6369485|lt=Smyers, Karen Ann|1=Karen Ann Smyers}}. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2275 The Traditional Festival in Urban Society], 1975, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/search.php?type=1&term=Bunrei&andor=AND Bunrei], Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved on July 8, 2008

{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
== Evolution of the ''kanjō'' process == ''Kanjō'' was originally a {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} term and later entered Shinto vocabulary.<ref name="Smyers147">Smyers (1999:235)</ref> A ''kanjō'' was the request of the {{ill|The Buddha|en|The Buddha|qid=Q9441|lt=The Buddha|1=The Buddha}}'s sermon with a sincere heart, and later came to mean the urging of a {{ill|Nyorai|en|Nyorai|qid=Q17052684|lt=buddha|1=Nyorai}} or {{ill|Bosatsu|en|Bosatsu|qid=Q23925162|lt=bodhisattva|1=Bosatsu}} to remain in this world to preach and save other human beings.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The concept then evolved further to mean the act (and the actual words) of asking buddhas or bodhisattvas to descend to the altar during a Buddhist service.<ref name="Smyers147"/> In Japan, the word gradually assumed the present meaning of enshrinement of a buddha or ''kami'' in a building for the first time.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
==The ''kanjō'' process == Before it can be transferred to its new location the ''kami'' must be divided.{{fact|date=August 2018}} The division sub-process and the divided spirit itself are called {{nihongo|''bunrei''|分霊}}, {{nihongo|''go-bunrei''|御分霊}} or {{nihongo|''wakemitama''|分霊}}.<ref name="Smyers147"/>
The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}} is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a {{ill|Kitsune|en|Kitsune|qid=Q692111|lt=fox|1=Kitsune}} hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}} ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to {{ill|Ono no Takamura|en|Ono no Takamura|qid=Q1139928|lt=Ono no Takamura|1=Ono no Takamura}}'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ({{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|qid=Q71699|lt=Aomori|1=Aomori Prefecture}}) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''. At {{ill|Toyokawa Inari|en|Toyokawa Inari|qid=Q3080596|lt=Toyokawa Inari|1=Toyokawa Inari}}, the worshiper can buy a statue and then participate in the ceremony, called ''[[kaigen]]'', to animate it.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
When one of Inari's forms is re-enshrined with a different name, it may also be worshiped for a specific function.<ref name="Smyers156"/> All the new functions are thereafter assumed to be specialties of the ''kami'', particularly in case of a great success in the re-enshrinement, even when those functions are very far from its original nature, as for instance fishing in Inari's case.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Smyers, Karen Ann. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2588 “My own Inari”: Personalization of the deity in Inari worship], 1996. Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * Sonoda, Minoru. [https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2275 The Traditional Festival in Urban Society], 1975, Japanese Journal of Religious Studies Online Archive, retrieved on July 22, 2020 * [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/search.php?type=1&term=Bunrei&andor=AND Bunrei], Encyclopedia of Shinto, retrieved on July 8, 2008

{{Short description|Ritual enshrinement of a Kami}} {{for|the Buddhist ritual (灌頂)| Abhiseka}} [[File:Kanjo ritual implements, Kamakura-Edo period, 13th-17th century, crown - Tokyo National Museum - DSC05208.JPG|thumb|right|''Kanjō'' ritual crown, Kamakura-Edo period (13th-17th century)]]
{{nihongo|'''Kanjō'''|勧請}} in [[Shinto]] terminology indicates a propagation process through which a ''[[kami]]'', previously divided through a process called ''[[bunrei]]'', is invited to another location and there re-enshrined.<ref name="Smyers147"/>

The process of propagation, described by Shinto priests as akin to the lighting of a candle from another already lit, leaves the original ''kami'' intact in its original place and therefore does not alter any of its properties.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The resultant ''wakemitama'' has all the qualities of the original and is therefore both living and permanent.<ref name="Smyers147"/> The process is used often, for example during ''[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]'' (Shinto festivals) to animate temporary shrines called {{nihongo|''mikisho''|神酒所}} and their portable versions, called ''[[mikoshi]]''.<ref name="sonoda12">Sonoda (1975:12)</ref>
=== ''Inari kanjō'' === {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}} is the ''kami'' that has been subjected to ''kanjō'' more often than any other,<ref name="Smyers156">Smyers (1999: 156-160)</ref> and is therefore a good illustration of the process.
The transfer does not necessarily take place from a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} to another: the new location can be a privately owned object or a ''[[kamidana]]'' ("god-shelf", or altar) within an individual house. The case is recorded of Inari being re-enshrined in a {{ill|Kitsune|en|Kitsune|qid=Q692111|lt=fox|1=Kitsune}} hole<ref name="Smyers156"/><ref>The fox is Inari's symbol.</ref> In fact, the first recorded {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}} ''kanjō'', in 842, involved the ''kami'''s transfer to {{ill|Ono no Takamura|en|Ono no Takamura|qid=Q1139928|lt=Ono no Takamura|1=Ono no Takamura}}'s scepter.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The ''kami'' was then transported to Mutsu no Kuni ({{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|qid=Q71699|lt=Aomori|1=Aomori Prefecture}}) by its owner.<ref name="Smyers156"/> Some years later, he returned to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, and Aomori's people asked him to leave the ''kami'' behind, which he did in what would become Takekoma Inari.<ref name="Smyers156"/>
In 1194, {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} [[:en:Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] decided that only [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] could perform any of the parts of the Inari ''kanjō'', however abuses were rampant that the shrine started providing an authenticity certificate with each divided spirit.<ref name="Smyers156"/> The process was briefly outlawed nationwide during the [[:en:Meiji Era|Meiji Era]], but was reinstated by popular demand. Nowadays, most large Inari shrines will perform it for a fee, sometimes set by the shrine or left to the discretion of the worshiper.<ref name="Smyers156"/> As of 1990, Fushimi Inari Shrine had performed it eighty thousand times for private citizens. <ref name="Smyers156"/> The faithful are often given the option to give a personal name to their personal ''kami''.
